stem: Find the length of the arc of the curve \( y = \dfrac{x^{[[n1]]}}{[[d1]]} + \dfrac{1}{[[d2]]\,x^{[[nm1]]}} \) from \( x=[[a]] \) to \( x=[[b]] \).
solution: Here \( y' = \dfrac{x^{[[n]]}}{2} - \dfrac{x^{-[[n]]}}{2} \), so \( 1+(y')^{2} = \left(\dfrac{x^{[[n]]}}{2}+\dfrac{x^{-[[n]]}}{2}\right)^{2} \) is a perfect square. Thus the arc length is \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}\left(\dfrac{x^{[[n]]}}{2}+\dfrac{x^{-[[n]]}}{2}\right)dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This evaluates \( y([[b]])-y([[a]]) \) (the change in the function itself), not the arc length.
This plugs the endpoints directly into the speed function and subtracts, instead of integrating it.
This doubles the correct arc length.
This halves the correct arc length.
This is off by one from the correct arc length.

stem: Find the length of the line segment along \( y = [[m]]x + ([[c]]) \) from \( x=[[a]] \) to \( x=[[b]] \), computed as an arc length integral.
solution: Here \( y' = [[m]] \) is constant, so the speed \( \sqrt{1+(y')^{2}} = \sqrt{1+[[m]]^{2}} \) is also constant, and the arc length is \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}\sqrt{1+[[m]]^{2}}\,dx = \sqrt{1+[[m]]^{2}}\,([[b]]-[[a]]) = [[answer]] \) — matching the distance formula.
distractor_rationales: This is just the horizontal run \( [[b]]-[[a]] \), ignoring the slope's contribution to the true (slanted) length.
This halves the correct arc length.
This uses \( |[[m]]| \) alone as the speed, forgetting the \( +1 \) under the square root.
This doubles the correct arc length.
This is off by one from the correct arc length.

stem: The line \( y=[[m]]x+[[c]] \), from \( x=[[a]] \) to \( x=[[b]] \), is revolved about the \( x \)-axis. Find the surface area of the resulting (frustum-shaped) solid.
solution: The surface area of revolution about the \( x \)-axis is \( 2\pi\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} y\sqrt{1+(y')^{2}}\,dx \). Here \( y=[[m]]x+[[c]] \) and \( y'=[[m]] \), so this is \( 2\pi\sqrt{1+[[m]]^{2}}\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}([[m]]x+[[c]])\,dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of \( 2\pi \).
This computes a volume-style washer difference instead of a surface area.
This drops the arc-length factor \( \sqrt{1+[[m]]^{2}} \) entirely.
This doubles the correct surface area.
This is off by \( \pi \) from the correct surface area.

stem: The curve \( y=\cosh(x) \), from \( x=[[a]] \) to \( x=[[b]] \), is revolved about the \( x \)-axis. Find the surface area of the resulting solid.
solution: Since \( y'=\sinh(x) \), the identity \( 1+\sinh^{2}(x)=\cosh^{2}(x) \) makes the speed \( \sqrt{1+(y')^{2}}=\cosh(x) \). The surface area is \( 2\pi\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}\cosh(x)\cdot\cosh(x)\,dx = 2\pi\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}\cosh^{2}(x)\,dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of \( 2\pi \).
This computes \( 2\pi \) times the plain ARC LENGTH \( \int\cosh(x)\,dx \), forgetting to multiply by the radius \( y=\cosh(x) \) again.
This computes a volume-style washer difference instead of a surface area.
This doubles the correct surface area.
This is off by \( \pi \) from the correct surface area.
